episodes.title: The Guyana of It All: Why Jim Jones Chose the Jungle
episodes.description: Before Jim Jones ever set foot in South America, Guyana was already a country ripe for exploitation. In this bonus context episode, we cover the political climate, Forbes Burnham, and why a remote stretch of jungle became the perfect place for a cult to disappear. You cannot understand Jonestown without understanding Guyana first.

episodes.title: Marceline: The Woman Whose Name Was on Every License | Peoples Temple Pt. 5
episodes.description:
Last week I told you what the Peoples Temple was actually delivering. Tonight I am going to tell you who was holding it up.

Her name was Marceline Baldwin Jones, R.N. She was the registered nurse on every single one of those care home licenses. She was the operational backbone of every single program the Peoples Temple ever ran. She was the mother of seven children, most of them adopted, most of them of different races than her, in what she and Jim called their Rainbow Family. She was the one the staff called Mother. She was the one the residents loved. She was the woman who made the help work.

And she paid for it with everything.

Tonight we are going to talk about her childhood in Indiana, her nursing training, the day she met Jim Jones at Reid Memorial Hospital in 1948 when she was 21 years old, the courtship, the marriage, the children, the move to California, and the slow closing of the trap her husband built around her using the very people she had spent her life saving. We are going to talk about the mistresses he installed in her household. The catharsis sessions. The public humiliations. The way he made leaving him feel like abandoning everyone in her care. And we are going to talk about why she stayed anyway.

Marceline is not the wife in this story. Marceline is the engine. Get her right, and the rest of the series finally makes sense.

Sources: jonestown.sdsu.edu. Stephan Jones interviews. Jim Jones Jr. interviews. Tim Reiterman's reporting in Raven. The Moore Family Papers.

#PeoplesTemple #Jonestown #MarcelineJones #TrueCrime #CultHistory

episodes.title: Who Was Edith Roller? The Woman Before the Couch | Peoples Temple Pt. 3
episodes.description:
If you only know Edith Roller as "a Jonestown victim," you do not know her at all.

Part 3 is the humanizing hour. Tonight we go all the way home. Edith Florence Roller, born in Colorado in 1915. The oldest of three sisters. The bookish one. The one who watched her country fall apart in the Great Depression when she was 14 years old and decided that someday somebody had better fix it. The college student in the 1930s reading Marx for the first time. The Foreign Service officer in Karachi, Pakistan in the 1950s. The community college English teacher in San Francisco in the 1960s and 1970s. The leftist. The feminist. The civil rights sympathizer. The woman with a whole life.

We are also meeting four other women tonight, by name, who walked into the Peoples Temple for the same reasons Edith did: Carolyn Layton, Annie Moore, Christine Miller, and Laurie Efrein. Educated. Idealistic. Politically conscious. Not lonely. Not naive. The cult-catches-lonely-women theory is bad sociology and worse history. The cult catches you through your strengths, not your weaknesses, and tonight you are going to understand why.

Sources: SDSU Alternative Considerations archive at jonestown.sdsu.edu. Statements from Dorothy Roller (Edith's surviving sister) in the FBI files. The work of Rebecca Moore, sister of Carolyn Layton and Annie Moore, who has been the central archivist of Peoples Temple history for over forty years.

Say their names in the comments. All five of them.

#PeoplesTemple #Jonestown #EdithRoller #CarolynLayton #AnnieMoore #ChristineMiller #TrueCrime

episodes.title: The Boat to Jonestown: What Edith Roller Saw on the River | Peoples Temple Pt. 2
episodes.description:
We left Edith in Georgetown. Tonight she gets on a boat.

In Part 2 of the Peoples Temple series, we ride the Kaituma River with Edith Roller as she travels from Georgetown to the place she has been told for years is paradise. We watch her arrive at the dock. We watch her see Jonestown for the first time. We walk past Lynetta Jones's grave on the property. We meet, through her own journal entries, the people we are going to spend the rest of this series with: Marceline Baldwin Jones, Carolyn Layton, Maria Katsaris, Sharon Amos, the children, the grandmothers, the kids on the basketball team. And we hear Jim Jones for the first time, only as a voice over a loudspeaker, slurring, sick, paranoid, far away.

By the end of the episode, Edith is going to record something in her journal that does not quite add up. A small thing. The first crack in the picture. And once that crack opens, we have to go back. We have to ask who she was before all this. We have to ask why a 63-year-old retired English professor with a State Department resume and a master's degree was riding a boat up a river in South America in the first place.

Sources: The Edith Roller Journals at jonestown.sdsu.edu, plus survivor interviews and Temple internal documents in the SDSU archive.

If you are new here, start with Part 1. We are building this slowly on purpose.

#PeoplesTemple #Jonestown #EdithRoller #TrueCrimeYouTube #CultHistory

episodes.title: The Stool Sample: A Woman Who Did Not Know She Was Dying | Peoples Temple Pt. 1
episodes.description:
In January of 1978, a 63-year-old retired English professor named Edith Roller submitted a stool sample to a health office in Georgetown, Guyana. She did it because she believed she was going somewhere better. She was right that it was somewhere different. She was wrong that it was somewhere better.

This is Part 1 of a 13-part series on the Peoples Temple, told a different way than you have probably ever heard it told. We are not starting in Indiana. We are not starting with Jim Jones. We are starting on a couch in Georgetown with a woman reading a book about psychic phenomena while a toddler is being toilet trained on the floor next to her, the shortwave radio crackles, and a Barbra Streisand movie plays on the television in the background. Because that is the door into this story. That is the room we have to walk into first if we are ever going to understand any of the rest of it.

Tonight we sit with Edith. We meet the people in the house. We let the small details land. And by the end of the hour, you will understand why the next 12 episodes have to exist.

Source archive: jonestown.sdsu.edu (the Edith Roller Journals project, maintained by Rebecca Moore and the Alternative Considerations of Jonestown and Peoples Temple research initiative at San Diego State University).

If this episode reaches you, leave Edith's name in the comments. I read every one.

#PeoplesTemple #Jonestown #EdithRoller #TrueCrime #CultDocumentary

episodes.title: THE MACHINE THAT BUILT THE CULT: How Jim Jones Became a San Francisco Kingmaker
episodes.description: Bonus episode. Before Jonestown, Jim Jones ran the most efficient turnout operation in California politics, and every Democrat in the state took the meeting. We are talking Tammany Hall, the Burton machine, George Moscone winning the mayor's race by 4,400 votes, Harvey Milk writing a letter to Jimmy Carter defending Jones, Rosalynn Carter's thank you note, Walter Mondale on the campaign plane, Willie Brown comparing Jones to MLK and Einstein and Chairman Mao at a testimonial dinner, and the Assistant DA who was also the Temple's lawyer. This is the part of the Jonestown story the documentaries skip.

episodes.title: The Help: What the Peoples Temple Was Actually Delivering | Pt. 4
episodes.description:
Tonight I am not going to say a single bad word about the Peoples Temple. Not one.

For the next hour, I am their lawyer. I am going to put their best foot forward. I am going to read the licenses. I am going to give you the addresses. I am going to walk you through what the church was actually doing in the Bay Area between 1965 and 1976. The nine state-licensed care homes. The foster placements. The drug rehabilitation program. The free medical clinic. The free legal aid. The college scholarships. The bus rides to dialysis. The grocery distributions. The voter registration that put George Moscone in the mayor's office. The endorsements from Harvey Milk, Willie Brown, Mervyn Dymally, Rosalynn Carter, Angela Davis, Dennis Banks, and the Southern Christian Leadership Conference.

And I am going to ask you the question this entire series has been working up to. If your grandmother was poor in San Francisco in 1974, and her only options were a state-run nursing facility that smelled like urine, or a clean room in a Peoples Temple care home with a registered nurse on staff and somebody humming a hymn in the kitchen, what would you have done? Be honest with yourself. Look at the country we are sitting in right now in 2026. Then answer.

This is the episode that converts the audience. By the end of it, you are going to stop asking how anyone could have fallen for this and start asking how you would have known not to.

Sources: jonestown.sdsu.edu. State of California licensing records. The Peoples Forum archive. The 1977 SCLC Humanitarian Award presentation records.

#PeoplesTemple #Jonestown #CultHistory #TrueCrime #SocialJusticeHistory
